agent1_snippets.content: Rarely do patients with CLL/SLL who have a dormant course present with acute aggressive lymphadenopathy. They need an urgent lymph node or bone marrow biopsy to rule out Richter transformation into aggressive diffuse large B cell lymphoma and rarely Hodgkin lymphoma or T cell lymphomas.

agent1_snippets.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Fit Patients: Clinically fit patients with IGHV-mutated CLL and without 17p deletion or TP53 mutation may either choose targeted therapy or chemoimmunotherapy depending upon patient preference and Fitness assessment. Intensive Chemoimmunotherapy (FCR or BR) may be preferred by patients desiring a long treatment-free interval. Options include: Fludarabine, cyclophosphamide, rituximab (FCR for six cycles (younger patients). Bendamustine plus rituximab (BR) for six cycles (older patients). Ibrutinib as a single agent (all ages) or in combination with rituximab or obinutuzumab (younger patients). Fixed duration venetoclax plus obinutuzumab (all ages). Acalabrutinib as a single agent (all ages).

agent1_snippets.title: Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Children who are suspected of having acute lymphocytic leukemia should be referred to a pediatric center that specializes in cancer for evaluation and treatment. For children with Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia, induction therapy consists of anthracycline, vincristine, 1-asparaginase, and a corticosteroid. Today consolidation therapy is widely used and includes therapy with a variety of chemotherapeutic drugs with good results.

High-risk disease: IGHV unmutated (without 17p deletion and TP53 mutation): These patients do respond to initial treatment with chemoimmunotherapy; however, targeted agents are preferred due to an improvement in progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) benefit compared to other chemoimmunotherapy. Treatment of choice is strongly based on patient preference and patient comorbidities. Initial treatment options include: Single-agent ibrutinib until progression (all ages) Ibrutinib plus rituximab until progression (younger patients) Fixed duration venetoclax plus obinutuzumab (all ages, use in younger patients extrapolated from trials in older adults) Acalabrutinib until progression (all ages)

agent1_snippets.title: Leukemia -- Treatment / Management -- ALL
agent1_snippets.content: For patients with IGVH mutation who have a relatively good prognosis, chemotherapy with FCR (fludarabine, cyclophosphamide, rituximab) [42] or BR (bendamustine, rituximab) [43] can be attempted as patients would be able to achieve prolonged disease-free survival for over ten years. For high-risk patients with del17p / TP53 mutation, patients benefit significantly from targeted therapy with venetoclax (BCL-2 inhibitor) or Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib), either as a single agent or in combination with rituximab or obinutuzumab. [44] [45] [46] [47] Older patients with comorbidities tolerate BTK inhibitors better.

agent1_snippets.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Treatment / Management -- B. Patients with Active Disease
agent1_snippets.content: The main purpose of systemic therapy is for symptomatic relief, prolonged remission, and prolonged survival in patients. Symptomatic CLL is treated, and despite many recent advances, it remains an incurable disease. For those patients for whom treatment is considered, a pre-treatment assessment should be done, including gathering information about age and general health, other features such as TP53 abnormalities or adverse cytogenetics, relapsed disease, or treatment-naive status. The selection of initial therapy in patients with symptomatic/advanced CLL is based on the patient, patient preference, tumor characteristics, and therapy goals.  Various treatment approaches for CLL use different combinations of the following agents. These agents significantly differ in their rates of time to progression, measurable residual disease (MRD), complete remission, and toxicities of drugs. There have been studies comparing different treatment modalities, and an individualized approach needs to be considered. Hence, patient preference plays a vital role in the ultimate treatment decision. Various drugs utilized in the management of CLL include;

agent1_snippets.title: Leukemia -- Treatment / Management -- ALL
agent1_snippets.content: CLL: CLL runs its course in a more indolent fashion than all the other leukemic subtypes, with the patient's lifespan minimally impacted by the disease. Patients do not benefit from early treatment unless they meet the criteria for therapy. Patients with a rapid doubling time of lymphocytes, worsening cytopenias, increasing spleen size causing abdominal discomfort, and significant B symptoms (fatigue, night sweats, and weight loss) benefit from treatment. The most important determinant in treating CLL is knowing the IGVH mutation status and the presence of del17p and TP53 mutation. t(11:14) is often obtained to rule out mantle cell lymphoma.

agent1_snippets.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Treatment / Management -- D. Relapsed/Refractory CLL
agent1_snippets.content: It is important to confirm relapsed/refractory CLL histologically prior to restarting treatment.  Recurrent CLL in asymptomatic patients should be monitored for symptoms requiring treatment. Richter's transformation should specifically be excluded.  The treatment of choice at relapse depends upon the initial treatment used and the initial duration of response to that treatment.

agent1_snippets.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Treatment / Management -- C. Management of complications of CLL and its therapy
agent1_snippets.content: CLL/SLL is characterized by hypo/agammaglobulinemia, which increases the risk of infections, especially Staphylococcus , H. influenza , Pneumococcus , and herpes. These infections can be managed with antimicrobials for bacterial, fungal, or viral infections. For refractory infections in patients with hypogammaglobinemia, or for 2 or more severe infections within a timeframe of 6-month, monthly IVIG infusions are considered.

agent1_snippets.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Unfit Patients: The management of patients with limited functional status is informed by the genetic features of the tumor (17p deletion, TP53 mutation, and IGHV mutation status). Many clinically unfit patients with comorbidities may benefit from treatment with single-agent ibrutinib, fixed duration venetoclax plus obinutuzumab, single-agent acalabrutinib, and/or the combination of bendamustine plus rituximab. Chlorambucil plus obinutuzumab is a treatment option for IGHV-mutated CLL in older patients with comorbidities.

agent1_snippets.content: a. Very high-risk disease: 17p deletion and/or TP53 mutations: These patients are at high risk of not responding to initial treatment with chemoimmunotherapy, or relapsing immediately after achieving remission. Initial treatment options include: Single-agent ibrutinib until progression (all ages)–is preferred based upon good tolerability of the drug, longer follow-up, and high response rates. Ibrutinib plus rituximab until progression (younger patients)– studies have shown that the addition of rituximab improved outcomes in younger adults compared to older adults. [62] Fixed duration venetoclax plus obinutuzumab for one year (all ages) [63] [64] [63] Single-agent venetoclax until progression (all ages) Single-agent acalabrutinib until progression (all ages)

agent1_snippets.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Treatment / Management -- D. Relapsed/Refractory CLL
agent1_snippets.content: Ibrutinib is the first-line therapy to improve progression-free survival and response to treatment in relapsed/refractory CLL.  Other drugs of choice for relapsed CLL include idelalisib (a phosphoinositide 3'-kinase [PI3K] delta inhibitor), Alemtuzumab (a monoclonal antibody directed against CD52, a cellular marker found in CLL) and venetoclax (Bcl-2 inhibitor). Venetoclax is actively used in refractory CLL patients with 17p deletion. Venetoclax induces brisk apoptosis and occasionally induces tumor lysis syndrome. Anti-CD19 chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy has also been active in refractory cases. Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody (rituximab, ofatumumab, obinutuzumab) may briefly alleviate symptoms in relapsed/refractory CLL patients.

agent1_snippets.content: A combination of venetoclax plus obinutuzumab is particularly used in patients in whom ibrutinib is contraindicated, such as patients with multiple comorbidities (example, history of severe bleeding, hepatic impairment, or atrial fibrillation) or if on any anticoagulation medications.  Studies have shown that the combination of venetoclax plus obinutuzumab demonstrates higher PFS and OS when compared with chlorambucil plus obinutuzumab. [63] [64]

agent1_snippets.title: Cytarabine -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Cytarabine is a medication used in the management and treatment of leukemias and lymphomas. It belongs to the antimetabolic group of medications. This activity reviews the indications, action, and contraindications for cytarabine as a valuable agent in treating acute myeloid leukemia (and other leukemias). This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors (e.g., off-label uses, dosing, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, monitoring, relevant interactions) pertinent for members of the healthcare team in the treatment of patients with leukemia and related conditions.

Certain patients receiving no treatment have survival rates similar to that of a normal population; current treatment regimens cannot cure CLL except for allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HCT). Randomized control trials between immediate versus delayed treatments found no significant improvement in long-term survival with early treatment initiation. agent1_snippets.title: Cytarabine -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Cytarabine has both labeled and off-labeled indications.  Labeled indications of cytarabine include the treatment of acute lymphoblastic leukemia, remission induction therapy in acute myeloid leukemia in adult and pediatric patients, the treatment of chronic myeloid leukemia (blast phase), and prophylaxis and treatment of meningeal leukemia. Some of the many off-labeled indications of cytarabine in adults include refractory chronic lymphocytic leukemia and refractory or relapsed Hodgkin disease. It is also an option for non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma and primary central nervous system (CNS) lymphoma.

agent1_snippets.content: The presence of del 17p or TP53 mutations is associated with poor response to cytotoxic chemotherapeutic regimens, including purine analog-based regimens. However, there is clinical evidence supporting the efficacy of BTK inhibitors such as Ibrutinib and acalabrutinib and BCL-2 inhibitor venetoclax as monotherapy or in combination with anti-CD 20 monoclonal antibodies. agent1_snippets.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Treatment / Management -- D. Relapsed/Refractory CLL
agent1_snippets.content: Allogeneic Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation is rarely performed in CLL but has been used in refractory cases in appropriate patients. Those with a very aggressive form of the disease, such as patients with 17p deletion, especially in younger patients, HLA-matched donors, should be recommended to get bone marrow transplant. Those without a matched donor or older in age can be given a trial of ibrutinib. Palliative radiation therapy can be given to chemotherapy-resistant areas of lymphadenopathy, liver, and spleen.  Total body irradiation occasionally helps relieve symptoms temporarily.

FCR is contraindicated in older patients due to the high risk of infections, adverse events, and high risk of immunosuppression. [62] [68] [62]

agent1_snippets.content: ALL is divided into B or T lymphocyte variants based on the lymphoblast origin and the presence of >20% lymphoblasts in peripheral smear or BM. The presence or absence of the Ph chromosome is the most important molecular marker leading to therapeutic implications in treating ALL. The overall outcome depends upon the patient's response to induction therapy and the presence or absence of MRD (minimal residual disease) needing further therapies and BMT.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 326
agent2_snippets.content: Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Both ponatinib and its hydrochloride salt share the same indication, reflecting that the active compound is effective in this hematologic malignancy. Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
agent2_snippets.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
agent2_snippets.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

D: CLL

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
agent2_snippets.content: Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
agent2_snippets.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
agent2_snippets.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors—including dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib—are classified as protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; dasatinib, its anhydrous form and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors such as ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to the protein kinase inhibitor, antineoplastic and immunomodulating group, with ibrutinib specifically indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, exemplified by canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, are another subset of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Additional protein kinase inhibitors in this ATC subclass include cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin may be employed in the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these kinase‑targeted therapies illustrate how distinct protein kinase inhibitor subclasses are organized within broader antineoplastic categories and matched to specific hematologic and lymphoid malignancies, supporting precise, patient‑centered treatment strategies.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 89
agent2_snippets.content: The compound 4‑aminopyrazolo(3,4‑d)pyrimidine is classified as an anticholesteremic agent, and within this anticholesteremic category a subcategory called hematologic agents (hematologické látky) is defined. Hematologic agents include drugs such as zanubrutinib, filgrastim, and protamines. Zanubrutinib, together with ibrutinib, belongs to the class of Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors; BTK inhibitors are themselves a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that have antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Ibrutinib is indicated for the treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Filgrastim, also a hematologic agent, is used to stimulate white‑blood‑cell production, and protamines represent another type of hematologic agent. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors constitute another group of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are examples of EGFR inhibitors. A broader grouping labeled “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” also falls under the protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents class. This group includes cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin have therapeutic activity against acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Overall, the taxonomy links several targeted therapies—BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other protein kinase inhibitors—to their respective clinical indications in hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia, while also situating them within broader pharmacologic categories like anticholesteremic agents and protein kinase inhibitor classes.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
agent2_snippets.content: Imatinib is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor, meaning it belongs to the class of drugs that specifically target BCR‑ABL fusion proteins. BCR‑ABL fusion proteins serve as the molecular target for BCR‑ABL inhibitors; they are oncogenic drivers created by the Philadelphia chromosome translocation and are central to the pathogenesis of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By binding to and inhibiting these fusion proteins, imatinib and other BCR‑ABL inhibitors can suppress the abnormal signaling that promotes uncontrolled cell proliferation, thereby providing an effective targeted therapy for patients with these malignancies.
